method3.generated_article:
==Etymology==
The name Nishinoshima is translated as “western island”.[2] The island was first recorded in 1702 by the Spanish ship Rosario and was initially named Rosario Island before it was later renamed Nishinoshima.[3] The parenthetical “Ogasawara” identifies it as the Nishinoshima belonging to the Ogasawara Islands, an island chain south of the Japanese archipelago.[3]

==Geology==
Nishinoshima is an active andesitic volcanic island in the Ogasawara Islands, about 1,000 km south of Tokyo and 130 km west of Chichijima.[1][4] It lies on the Shichito-Iwojima Ridge of the Izu-Bonin arc, a north–south volcanic front parallel to the Ogasawara Trench, where volcanism is related to subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath the Philippine Sea Plate.[4] The island forms the emergent summit of a much larger submarine volcano about 30 km in diameter.[1] The volcanic edifice includes the island-forming main peak and two submarine peaks to the west and south, with a small cone on the northeast flank that may be a satellite cone.[4]

Before the major 21st-century enlargement of the island, Nishinoshima was described as about 650 m long, about 200 m wide, about 77,000 m2 in area, and 25 m high, with reefs on its northeast side.[1] The first recorded eruption began in May 1973 on the sea floor about 400 m east of the southern tip of the island and produced a new island by September of that year.[1] That eruption began with submarine phreatomagmatic explosions, migrated semicircularly, and involved five craters that emitted volcanic blocks and lava flows.[1] Although much of the new volcanic edifice was eroded, reworked deposits connected the old and new islands, and by 1982 sediment movement had closed the mouth of the intervening bay.[1] Ejecta from the 1973 eruption had SiO2 contents of 51.8 to 60.1 wt %, consistent with basaltic andesite to andesite compositions.[1]

Renewed eruptions from 2013 to 2020 were divided into four episodes, beginning with a two-year episode in 2013–2015 and followed by shorter episodes in 2017, 2018, and 2019–2020.[3] The 2013 eruption produced a new island that merged with the older Nishinoshima in late 2013, and the eruption continued until 2015.[2] Average effusion rates were estimated at about 2.0 × 105 m3 per day for the 2013–2015 episode and 1.5 × 105 m3 per day for the 2017 episode.[3] The fourth episode, lasting about 270 days in 2019–2020, marked a change from earlier activity and included Strombolian eruptions with lava effusion, lava-fountain activity, and later fine ash emissions probably generated by phreatomagmatic processes.[3][4]

Petrological study of submarine deposits from the 2020 eruption showed that Nishinoshima erupted basalt, basaltic andesite, andesite, and dacite during that event.[3] The andesites were similar to those erupted from the island since 1973, while the 2020 basalts resembled older basalts known from surrounding submarine knolls.[3] These results indicate that basaltic magmas may have been produced throughout the volcano’s history, but that their eruptive center shifted to the island summit in 2020.[3] Nishinoshima andesite primary magmas are interpreted to originate directly from the mantle by shallow, hydrous melting of plagioclase peridotite.[3]

==Ecology==
Nishinoshima is treated by researchers as a rare field site for observing primary succession and biological colonization on newly exposed oceanic island surfaces within the isolated Ogasawara archipelago.[3][4] The surrounding Ogasawara Islands are characterized by island-specific ecosystems, diverse biogeographic origins of colonists, and high concentrations of endemic and rare species, making Nishinoshima’s recolonization of interest for studies of adaptive radiation and speciation in isolation.[5] On Nishinoshima itself, ecological conditions are unstable because volcanic activity and marine erosion repeatedly alter available habitats and substrates, periodically removing or burying established biota.[5] Botanical colonization has been documented on the island, including early studies of plant invasion on newly formed volcanic land.[4] One documented plant was common purslane, Portulaca oleracea; before a later eruption, researchers collected living plants from Nishinoshima for laboratory cultivation, and the local population was reported to have disappeared from the island after the 2019 eruption.[2][4] Such losses illustrate that ecological succession on Nishinoshima can be repeatedly reset by physical disturbance rather than proceeding toward a stable terrestrial community.[2][5] Faunal and marine communities are also studied as part of the island’s colonization process, with monitoring focused on species arrival, ecosystem development, and coral-reef formation around the island.[3] Because of its remoteness, repeated creation of bare substrate, and location in an archipelago noted for ongoing evolutionary processes, Nishinoshima is regarded as a natural laboratory for ecosystem formation rather than as a site with a long-established terrestrial biota.[3][5]
